title: practical english usage - Part 37

We   often use the two present tenses in a similar way to give instructions, 3  instructions         and   demonstrations beautiful rhythm; Cambridge are looking a little disorganised...

Oxford are pulling slightly ahead of Cambridge now; they're rowing with a Harris passes back to Simms, nice ball - and Simms shoots!

Smith passes to Devaney, Devaney to Barnes - and Harris intercepts...

for instance, than in a commentary on a boat race.

There are more simple and fewer progressive tenses in a football commentary, describe them); the present progressive is used for longer actions and situations.

quicker actions and events (which are finished before the sentences that In commentaries, the use of tenses is similar. The  simple present is used for the 2 commentaries Chapter 2: Henry goes to Scotland and meets the Loch Ness Monster.

In Act I, Hamlet sees the ghost of his father. The ghost tells him  ...

The  simple present is common in summaries of plays, stories, etc.

There's this Scotsman, and he's walking through the jungle when he meets a grammar •  34   present tenses: advanced points For simple and progressive forms in older English, ►    318.10.

For progressive forms in general, ►    3.   For the 'distancing’  use of progressive forms, »*311.

For progressive forms with always and similar words (e.g. She's always losing her keys), ►    5.

I look forward to hearing from you. (Less formal: I'm looking forward to I enclose a recent photograph. (Less formal: I am enclosing...) We write to advise you ... (Less formal: We are writing to let you know...) in the simple present (more formal) or in the present progressive (less formal).

Some fixed phrases that are used in letters, emails, etc can be expressed either 1 formal     correspondence or My head aches,  My head is aching.

How do you feel?   H   are you feeling?

simple or progressive tenses without much difference of meaning.

Verbs that refer to physical feelings (e.g. feel, hurt, ache) can often be used in 6 I feel     / I'm  feeling There goes our bus.

Here comes your husband, (     Here is-eoming-. ..) Note the structures here comes... and there goes ...

5 Here    comes       ..., etc No doubt you all remember what Hamlet says about suicide.

It says in the paper that petrol's going up again.

Other people’ s statements, opinons, etc are often introduced with says.

I gather Daniel's looking for a job.

I see there's been trouble down at the factory.

I hear you're getting married. (= ’ I have heard ...') introductory expressions like I hear, I see, I gather, I understand (►  482).

The  simple present is used with a perfect kind of meaning (= 'ɪ have learnt’ ) in 4 I hear,     etc not He denies the accusation, (     He is denying...) not I swear that I will tell the truth... I agree, (     Jam-agreeing.) not 1 promise never to smoke again, (     Pm-premising-...) We   usually use the simple present in these cases.

Sometimes we  do things by saying special words (e.g. promising, agreeing).

3 / promise         .. ., etc Present     Tenses     Section 3 grammar •  Section 4  Talking about the Future This time tomorrow I'll be lying on the beach.

particular time. For details, and other uses of this tense, >41.

The  future progressive can be used to say that something will be in progress at a By next Christmas we'll have been here for eight years.

or achieved by a certain time. For details, ►    40.

We   can use the future perfect to say that something will be completed, finished other     ways    of  talking     about     the   future The train leaves at half past six tomorrow morning.

situations. For details, ►    37.

The  simple present can also be used to talk about the future, but only in certain simple     present:     the    train      leaves       ...

Shall I carry your bag?  I'll hit you if you do that again.

and announcements of decisions. For details, ►    79-80.

towards other people: they are common in offers, requests, threats, promises Will (and sometimes shall) are also used to express our intentions and attitudes Nobody will ever know the truth. I think Liverpool will win.
